choices: a condition the type checker enforces about attribute types
a condition that holds right after construction but may lapse afterward
a condition on internal state: established by the constructor, preserved by every method, assumed by each on entry
an assertion clients must check before calling each method

questions.prompt: class Team: members = [] — two instances each call self.members.append(...). The result:
choices: one shared list on the class: both instances (and all future ones) see every append
each instance silently gets its own list on first append
AttributeError — instances cannot reach class attributes
the second instance's append raises, because the list belongs to the first

questions.prompt: Define __eq__ on a class and leave __hash__ undefined. Your instances:
choices: hash by identity, as before
hash by value automatically
work as dict keys but compare by identity inside the dict
become unhashable — using one as a dict key raises TypeError
make attributes unreachable from outside the class
prevent accidental attribute collisions in subclasses — not to keep secrets
mark attributes as eligible for garbage collection sooner
speed up attribute lookup on hot paths

questions.prompt: Dynamic dispatch means the method that runs is chosen by:
choices: the runtime type of the receiver, at the moment of the call
the type declared or implied at the call site
the order in which the candidate classes were defined
the first isinstance check that matches
a square is not a rectangle, mathematically
Python forbids overriding inherited setters
Rectangle's contract lets width and height vary independently, and Square cannot honor that
Square fails to add any new behavior of its own

questions.prompt: LoggingDict(dict) overrides __setitem__ to log writes. LoggingDict(a=1), then d["b"] = 2, then d.update(c=3). How many writes were logged?
choices: 1 — the constructor and update write through internal paths that skip the override
2 — only the constructor bypasses the override
0 — dict never dispatches to subclass methods
the immediate parent of the class where it appears, always
the first base class listed in the class statement
object
the next class in the instance's MRO — which need not be the parent
